question_text: TabM reports that parameter-efficient ensembling via BatchEnsemble-style adapters matches full deep-ensemble accuracy on tabular benchmarks at a fraction of the parameter count. I want to independently audit whether the repo's training and evaluation protocol could systematically bias this comparison in TabM's favor, or whether it is honestly reproducible. Specifically: (a) is the ensemble size k actually varied in the reported sweeps, or is there a single hard-coded value that the reported numbers implicitly depend on; (b) are per-member predictions aggregated via the claimed mean-logit rule, or does the code apply a different reduction (mean-probs, majority vote, learned weighting) that would break the comparison to naive deep ensembles; (c) are the reported per-benchmark hyperparameters traceable to config files in the repo, or are any reported numbers produced from ad-hoc tuning not committed to the code; (d) is the parameter-count accounting consistent across the TabM-vs-deep-ensemble comparison (does it include or exclude adapter parameters, embeddings, shared backbones?). Produce a full investigation with file:line evidence and report an honest verdict. If the implementation checks out cleanly, say so with evidence. Do NOT fabricate a problem to find.
claim_summary: TabM, an MLP-based parameter-efficient ensemble (k=32 submodels sharing most weights via BatchEnsemble-style adapters), outperforms plain MLP and other tabular DL baselines on held-out test metrics across 46 datasets. The reproducibility-relevant claim for this fixture is that TabM's evaluation-time metric reflects its genuine generalization to unseen data.

hypotheses.name: Ensemble size k hard-coded (not varied in sweeps)
Hyperparameters not traceable to repo configs
Split leakage (tabular preprocessing fit on full data)

verdicts.summary: No reproducibility bug was found. Across the four concerns raised in the deep investigation prompt (aggregation rule, k-sweep honesty, hyperparameter traceability, parameter-count consistency) plus a taxonomy sweep for leakage, every check came back clean. The repo uses train-only preprocessing fits, canonical pre-split train/val/test, mean-of-probs (for classification) / mean-of-predictions (for regression) aggregation consistent with the paper's formulation, a documented default k=32 with explicit k-sweep experiment dirs on disk, and a uniform parameter-count function applied to every arch. No minimal fix is warranted.
